filename: The genetic basis of exercise motivation PTC bonus (1).pdf
source: The genetic basis of exercise motivation PTC bonus (1).pdf
strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
1

THE GENETIC BASIS OF
EXERCISE MOTIVATION

3

Genes associated with exercise
In an effort to try to understand the mechanisms of heritability of exercise behavior,
researches have tried to find associated genomic locations in the human genome -
called quantitative trait loci (QTL). A QTL is basically a segment of a chromosome which
influences a quantitative phenotypic trait of a given organism, like height. If relevant
genomic regions are found, the next step is to identify single candidate genes
associated with physical activity.

While research has found a handful of genes that may contribute to physical activity,
there are 2 genes that have a solid base of research in this regard: dopamine receptor 1
(Drd1) and nescient helix loop helix 2 (Nhlh2).

Dopamine receptor 1 (Drd1)
Alterations of the neurotransmitter dopamine have been found in many diseases that
involve physical activity (e.g. Parkinson´s disease, ADHD). Thus it makes sense to
conclude that dopamine genes have functional relevance in this regard. Indeed, there
are multiple lines of research showing that the dopamine gene Drd1 is involved in the
regulation of physical activity (e.g. Rhodes et al., 2003).

Nescient helix loop helix 2 (Nhlh2)
This gene is associated with leptin, and its encoded protein is a precursor to the
formation of endorphins which regulate hunger and pain. They are also involved in
experiencing the "runners high." It has been shown that mice display reduced wheel
running activity of approximately 50 % when Nhlh2 has been removed.

Side note: This review showed that there are potentially distinct genetic influences on
physical activity and physical inactivity. For example, the ACE (angiotensin-converting
enzyme) genotype was strongly associated with physical inactivity.

4

The biological motivation to exercise
In earlier stages of evolution, we still had to chase down a mammoth by ourselves in
order to have something to eat for dinner. The motivation to engage in such physically
demanding behavior was strongly linked to survival.

We know that more endurance type exercise can lead to feelings of euphoria and a
reduction in anxiety. From an evolutionary perspective, it makes sense to believe that
these mechanisms are in place to motivate us to engage in such energetically costly
behavior.

Some research has even shown that exercise can have similar effects to drugs, in that it
can be so extremely habit-forming that it reaches the point of addiction (Werme et al.,
2000).
Central neural reward pathways that are involved in the formation of addiction also
seem to play a big role in voluntary physical activity and motivation.

Dopamine, often called the "feel excited" or "wanting" neurotransmitter, is critical in this
regard. For example, when we get a reward after certain operations (these can be
actions, thoughts, cues, compliments, etc.), we get a spike in dopamine levels and feel
really good. We "learn" that a specific operation leads to a good feeling and it becomes
more likely that we engage in the same or similar actions in the future. As such, it is
likely critical in fostering motivational behavior.

Indeed, Hamid et al. (2015) showed that "phasic (instant) changes in dopamine levels
immediately affect willingness to engage in work, supporting the idea that subsequent
dopamine fluctuations promptly influence motivational decision-making."

We also know that a positive affective response after voluntary exercise can aid in
building exercise motivation over time.

5

On that account, Flack et al. (2017) found that for people who meet the recommended
guidelines for the quantity of aerobic activity and muscle-strengthening activity, exercise
is more reinforcing (+58% and +54%, respectively). The reinforcing value of different
modalities of exercise seems distinct, as the researchers found that an individual´s
preference and tolerance for exercise predicted the reinforcing value of resistance
training but not aerobic training.

Ability and motivation of voluntary exercise
Biologically speaking, exercise behavior has an ability factor and a motivational factor.
Both factors are genetically determined to a certain extent. Multiple lines of research
show that certain individuals respond more favorably to exercise, while others, even
though possibly highly motivated, show diminished adaptations. Ability to exercise and
motivation to do so might also influence each other, as it is plausible that high aptitude
leads to higher levels of motivation.

Bryan et al (2007) proposed a trans-disciplinary model to explain exercise behavior and
showed empirical support for it. In their model (see graphic below), the physiological
effects (body temperature changes etc.) influenced the subjective psychological
experience of exercise (changes in mood, perceived exertion), which in turn are
important determinants for the motivation to exercise. Genetic factors modulate the
physiological response to exercise and/or subjective experiences. For example,
individuals who cope better with exercise physiologically (e.g. better modulation of body
temperature) will experience more immediate psychological benefits (increased mood,
less perceived exertion, etc.). This is followed by increased motivation to exercise,
leading to actual exercise behavior, which again repeats the whole process and
reinforces the positive psychological experiences with exercise.
headings: Ability and motivation of voluntary exercise

pages.text:
6

This study showed that people who experience a more positive mood after exercise are
also more likely to show intentions to exercise in the future. They also showed that
when engaging in aerobic exercise, lower ratings of perceived exertion, lower heart rate
and lower increases in body temperature were significant predictors of positive mood
after exercise.

Interestingly, they also identified the brain derived neurotropic factor (BDNF) gene as
one that had a direct influence on ratings of perceived exertion, heart rate and positive
mood in response to a bout of aerobic activity. This suggests that the mood someone
experiences may be influenced by the interaction between physiological changes and
genetic variation (ref).

The psychology of motivation
One of the most prevalent theories in psychology to explain different types of motivation
is the model by Deci and Ryan, namely Self-Determination-Theory (SDT), which has a
solid empirical base. It describes a continuum which ranges from amotivation (no

7

motivation at all) to true intrinsic motivation (where people pursue goals for the sake of
interest, enjoyment etc.) with different forms of extrinsic (controlled from the "outside")
motivation in the middle.

According to Berger et al. (2002), approximately 50% of people who start an exercise
program will drop out after the first 6 months. What is it that distinguishes those who
exercise regularly from those who don´t?

This review of empirical studies on motivation and exercise by Texeira et al. (2012)
showed that there is a positive relation between more autonomous forms of motivation
and engagement in exercise. It is noteworthy that the adoption of exercise behavior was
strongly predicted by motivation in the form of identified regulation; people pursue a
behavior because the outcomes are valued as important to someone´s personal goals.
Intrinsic motivation had no predictive value in this regard. However, intrinsic motivation
was more predictive for long-term exercise adherence.

This suggests that the motives by which people start a regular exercising program are
not necessarily the same as what keeps them motivated in the long term, and that the

8

type of motivation can change over time. We´ll discuss what tools you can use with
clients who aren´t initially intrinsically motivated in a bit.

Accordingly, Friederichs et al. (2015) suggests that extrinsic motivators, such as peer
pressure, external rewards etc. can be involved in the initiation of regular exercise,
whereas more autonomous types of motivation, like enjoyment, are associated with
maintaining exercise in the long term.

To illustrate this, Viljoen and Christie (2015) put post-menopausal, sedentary women on
a 24-week progressive resistance training program in small group sessions 3 times per
week. They found that the motivating factors at the beginning of the study were the
routine itself and supervision; later in the program, factors such as social cohesion of
the group setting and a desire to maintain "wellness" were the primary reasons for
adherence.

The importance and power of self control
In short: experiencing self-control is important for happiness and well-being in everyday
life. This seems to be due to avoiding and dealing with motivational conflict.

In a study of regular exercisers, the trait of self-control was found to be the mediating
factor between motivation to exercise and subjective well-being. It is interesting to note
that in this study, intrinsic motivation to exercise was positively related to subjective
well-being. Extrinsic (controlled) motivation to exercise and no motivation to exercise
were both negatively related to subjective well-being.

According to the theory of planned behavior, people engage in a behavior if their
intentions to do so are high enough. In this theoretical framework, intentions are linked
to social norms, attitudes and perceived behavioral control.
headings: The importance and power of self control

pages.text:
9

There is an abundance of research showing that intentions to exercise are indeed a big
predictor for actual exercise behavior. For example, Bryan and Rocheleau (2002)
looked at the predictors of why people engage in either resistance training and/or
aerobic training. They found that while the reasons for exercising are similar between
the modalities, perceived behavioral control was strongly linked to exercise intentions
for resistance training. A big portion of the participants in this study reported that they
did not engage in resistance training because they simply did not know how.

Personality and exercise
Personality traits are thought of as relatively stable patterns of thoughts and behavior.
For example, it is known that certain personality traits, such as extroversion, are
associated with an increased tolerance for exteroceptive stimuli (in the environment)
while introverts are more tolerant to and prefer interoceptive stimuli (e.g. sensations,
thoughts etc.). It has been hypothesized that these traits could be related to exercise
behavior. However, findings in this regard have been mixed and there has not been a
clear trend.

Ekkekakis et al. (2005) measured more exercise-specific personality traits, namely
"preference for exercise intensity" and "tolerance for exercise intensity." They defined
these traits as a "predisposition towards a specific preference for an exercise intensity"
and "the ability to continue exercising even when the activity becomes uncomfortable or
unpleasant, respectively."

Indeed, this research in college women shows that people who engage in more frequent
strenuous exercise are more tolerant of intensity and even prefer it, but it is unclear
whether exercise intensity tolerance can develop over time or shape the preference for
a certain type of exercise.

10

Taken together, these findings suggest that there are personality traits that relate
specifically to exercise and modality; these traits are at least partly heritable, much like
other traits, such as extroversion.

11

Getting someone motivated to exercise
While certain individuals are genetically predisposed to be more active and engage in
sports/regular exercise, implementing regular exercise can be a big step for someone
who is sedentary.

It ultimately comes down to implementing a new habit in someone's life.
It is helpful to set a clear goal, self-monitor and have a good plan to ensure that the right
actions are being executed to achieve it, so that you don´t have to make decisions on
the fly.

Evidence shows that implementation intentions, which are simple if-then plans, are
effective for physical activity, especially with adequate barrier management.
In practice this could mean:
• Goal: daily exercise
• Plan: when, where and how you are going to do it
• Identify possible obstacles (barriers) and how to cope with them in advance

It is important that the prior outcome expectancies of the goal(s) are realistic to ensure
satisfaction. A behavior is more likely to stick if it has short-term positive outcomes,
which maximizes the chance of it becoming a habit over time.

To foster intrinsic motivation, it is useful to give positive feedback, praise and
encouragement. The support of a mentor to whom the person can relate is also helpful.
With progressively more intense regular exercise, it is speculative that tolerance and
enjoyment for exercise intensity increase over time. There is more and more emerging
evidence suggesting that long-term adherence is related to exercise enjoyment.

12

Conclusions
• There is a strong genetic basis that predisposes certain individuals to engage in
more exercise behavior. Most involved genes are linked to the neural reward
pathway.

• Personality traits like preference and tolerance to exercise intensity likely play a
role in which form of exercise someone prefers.

• The motivational factors that lead a person to initiate a regular exercise program
might differ from the motivation to continue a program in a later stage. It is
probable that people who are genetically predisposed to show less exercise
behavior need more routine and structure at the beginning of an exercise
program.

• Certain motivational tools, like implementation intentions and positive
affirmations, might help to initiate regular exercising and to foster a sense of self
control. Over time, this may translate to a more internalized type of motivation.

all_headings: Ability and motivation of voluntary exercise
The importance and power of self control
